agent_output: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"Order cancellation feature for an order management system. Three stakeholders (Product, Finance, Operations) have provided requirements that govern when and whether an order may be cancelled.","bounded_context":"Order Management / Order Lifecycle","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"Order","definition":"A customer's request to purchase goods, progressing through a lifecycle from placement to shipment."},{"term":"Cancellation","definition":"The act of voiding an order, preventing it from being fulfilled and shipped."},{"term":"Invoice","definition":"A financial document generated against an order that locks in pricing. Finance considers invoicing irreversible."},{"term":"Shipment","definition":"The physical dispatch of goods to the customer; represents a point-of-no-return in the PM's model."},{"term":"Order Placement","definition":"The moment a customer confirms an order. Operations states invoicing occurs at this point."}]},"business_rules":[{"id":"BR-1","rule":"An order can be cancelled at any time before it ships.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"This is a business policy from Product defining the cancellation window. A VP could narrow or widen it.","testable_assertion":"Given an order in any pre-shipment state, a cancellation request succeeds."},{"id":"BR-2","rule":"Orders that have been invoiced cannot be cancelled; invoicing is irreversible.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Finance treats the invoice as an immutable financial record. Cancelling an invoiced order would violate accounting integrity and potentially regulatory/audit requirements.","testable_assertion":"Given an order with an associated invoice, a cancellation request is rejected."},{"id":"BR-3","rule":"Orders are invoiced at the time of placement to lock in pricing.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"This is an operational policy choice about when to invoice. It could be changed to invoice at shipment or at a different trigger point.","testable_assertion":"Given a newly placed order, an invoice record is created synchronously during the placement process."}],"acceptance_criteria":[],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"Customer places an order and immediately requests cancellation","expected_behavior":"CONTRADICTED: BR-1 says this should succeed (order has not shipped). BR-2 + BR-3 say this must fail (order was invoiced at placement, and invoiced orders cannot be cancelled)."},{"scenario":"Order placed but shipment delayed for days","expected_behavior":"CONTRADICTED: PM expects a long cancellation window (until shipment). Finance and Ops rules make the order uncancellable from the moment it exists."},{"scenario":"Partial invoicing or split orders","expected_behavior":"Undefined. If only part of an order is invoiced, the rules give no guidance on partial cancellation."}],"business_impact":{"financial":"If Finance's rule wins, customers cannot cancel orders ever, leading to increased refund/chargeback costs and potential regulatory exposure. If PM's rule wins, reversing invoices has accounting and audit implications.","operational":"Operations invoices at placement for pricing certainty. Allowing cancellation post-invoice requires a credit note or void workflow that does not currently exist (or is not mentioned).","user":"Customers expect to cancel orders before shipment. If cancellation is effectively impossible, customer satisfaction and retention will suffer significantly.","risk":"Implementing any interpretation without resolving this contradiction risks either financial/audit violations (reversing irreversible invoices) or a broken product promise (cancellation feature that never works).","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"Customers","impact":"Cannot cancel orders at all if Finance + Ops rules take precedence, contradicting reasonable expectations set by the product."},{"group":"Finance / Accounting","impact":"If PM's rule takes precedence, invoiced orders get cancelled, violating their stated invariant and creating accounting reconciliation problems."},{"group":"Operations","impact":"May need to change invoicing timing if the contradiction is resolved by decoupling invoice from placement."},{"group":"Customer Support","impact":"Will bear the brunt of customer complaints if cancellation is impossible, or will need new workflows for credit notes if invoice reversal is introduced."},{"group":"Legal / Compliance","impact":"Depending on jurisdiction, inability to cancel orders may violate consumer protection laws (e.g., cooling-off periods)."}]},"confidence":{"level":40,"reasoning":"The three requirements contain a direct logical contradiction that makes it impossible to define correct behavior. BR-1 guarantees cancellability before shipment, but BR-2 + BR-3 together guarantee uncancellability from the moment of placement. No implementation can satisfy all three rules simultaneously. Confidence is low because the spec is internally inconsistent.","high_confidence_areas":["The contradiction is real and unambiguous — not a matter of interpretation","Invoice immutability is a legitimate financial constraint","Invoicing at placement is a stated operational fact"],"low_confidence_areas":["Which rule should yield — this is a business decision, not a technical one","Whether 'invoicing' in Finance's sense is the same event as 'invoicing' in Operations' sense","Whether a credit note or void mechanism exists that could resolve the tension"],"assumptions":["All three statements are current and authoritative for their respective domains","Invoice as used by Finance and Operations refers to the same concept","No existing credit note or invoice void process exists that would decouple cancellation from invoice reversal"]},"escalations":[{"type":"contradiction","description":"The three requirements are mutually exclusive. (1) PM says orders are cancellable before shipment. (2) Finance says invoiced orders are never cancellable. (3) Ops says orders are invoiced at placement. Combining (2) and (3): orders are uncancellable from the instant they exist. This directly contradicts (1). No implementation can satisfy all three simultaneously.","affected_stakeholders":["Product Management","Finance","Operations","Customers","Customer Support","Legal/Compliance"],"options":["A: Change invoicing timing — invoice at shipment instead of placement, preserving both the cancellation window and invoice immutability. Ops loses pricing lock-in at placement.","B: Introduce a credit note / void mechanism — allow cancellation to issue a credit note against the invoice rather than reversing it. Finance's immutability is preserved in letter (invoice stays) while PM's cancellation is honored in spirit.","C: Redefine 'cancellation' — a cancelled order triggers a credit note and a new negative invoice, keeping the original invoice intact. Requires Finance to accept this as compliant.","D: PM narrows the rule — orders are only cancellable before invoicing, which effectively means never under current Ops policy. Product feature becomes meaningless."],"recommendation":"Option B or C: Introduce a credit note mechanism so that invoices remain immutable financial records while orders can still be cancelled before shipment. This respects Finance's audit trail, preserves Ops' pricing lock-in, and delivers the PM's cancellation feature. All three stakeholders must agree on the credit note workflow before acceptance criteria can be defined."}],"rejection_reasons":[{"violation":"Cannot define acceptance criteria: the requirements as stated are internally contradictory.","business_rule_broken":"BR-1 (cancellable before shipment) is irreconcilable with BR-2 + BR-3 (uncancellable from placement) without a resolution path agreed upon by all stakeholders."}]}
